infobox.value: (近江大津宮, *Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya*) was an imperial palace built by [Emperor Tenchi](/wiki/Emperor_Tenchi "Emperor Tenchi") in [Asuka period](/wiki/Asuka_period "Asuka period") Japan in what is now the city of [Ōtsu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu "Ōtsu"), [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"), Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the **Ōtsu Palace** (大津宮, *Ōtsu-no-miya*), or **Shiga no Miyako** (志賀の都), it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the **Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya** (水海大津宮).[[1]](#cite_note-Shiseki-1)

(Redirected from [Ōtsu Palace](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu_Palace&redirect=no "Ōtsu Palace"))

Ōmi Ōtsu Palace

The **Ōmi Ōtsu Palace** (近江大津宮, *Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya*) was an imperial palace built by [Emperor Tenchi](/wiki/Emperor_Tenchi "Emperor Tenchi") in [Asuka period](/wiki/Asuka_period "Asuka period") Japan in what is now the city of [Ōtsu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu "Ōtsu"), [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"), Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the **Ōtsu Palace** (大津宮, *Ōtsu-no-miya*), or **Shiga no Miyako** (志賀の都), it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the **Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya** (水海大津宮).[[1]](#cite_note-Shiseki-1)
It was at this location that the [Ōmi Code](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Code "Ōmi Code") and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later *[ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō")* state. Its location was designated a [National Historic Site of Japan](/wiki/Monuments_of_Japan "Monuments of Japan") in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007[[2]](#cite_note-Bunka1-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi_%C5%8Ctsu_Palace&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 660 AD, during the reign of [Empress Saimei](/wiki/Empress_Saimei "Empress Saimei"), the Korean kingdom of [Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje") fell to an alliance of [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla") and [Tang China](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"). Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion and [Yamato's](/wiki/Yamato_Kingship "Yamato Kingship") foothold in the mainland. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the [Battle of Baekgang](/wiki/Battle_of_Baekgang "Battle of Baekgang") in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from [Asuka](/wiki/Asuka,_Nara "Asuka, Nara") to the shore of [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa") in [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province").

The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") coast, and the location was on the major ancient [Tōsandō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dsand%C5%8D "Tōsandō") and [Hokurikudō](/wiki/Hokurikud%C5%8D "Hokurikudō") highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system to counter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*, there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.

Emperor Tenchi died in 671. He had originally designated his brother, [Prince Ōama](/wiki/Prince_%C5%8Cama "Prince Ōama"), as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, [Prince Ōtomo](/wiki/Emperor_K%C5%8Dbun "Emperor Kōbun"). This led to the [Jinshin War](/wiki/Jinshin_War "Jinshin War"), during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the [Emperor Tenmu](/wiki/Emperor_Tenmu "Emperor Tenmu"), and moved the capital back to Asuka.[[1]](#cite_note-Shiseki-1)

In the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period"), the capital was moved to [Heijō-kyō](/wiki/Heij%C5%8D-ky%C5%8D "Heijō-kyō"), and the [provincial capital of Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Kokuch%C5%8D "Ōmi Kokuchō") was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of the ruins of Ōtsu Palace was named Furutsu (古津), meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [Emperor Kanmu](/wiki/Emperor_Kanmu "Emperor Kanmu"). However, over time, the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") and the [Taishō era](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_era "Taishō era"), there was a strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of [Sūfuku-ji](/wiki/S%C5%ABfuku-ji_(%C5%8Ctsu) "Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)") and the [Minamishigachō temple ruins](/wiki/Minamishigach%C5%8D_temple_ruins "Minamishigachō temple ruins") amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [roof tiles](/wiki/Roof_tile "Roof tile"), very little physical evidence survived.

In 1974, during [rescue archaeology](/wiki/Rescue_archaeology "Rescue archaeology") on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [archaeological excavations](/wiki/Archaeological_excavation "Archaeological excavation") have found [Sue](/wiki/Sue_ware "Sue ware") and [Haji ware](/wiki/Haji_ware "Haji ware") pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the [Naniwa Palace](/wiki/Naniwa-ky%C5%8D "Naniwa-kyō") which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west. The site has been preserved as an [archaeological park](/wiki/Archaeological_park "Archaeological park").[[1]](#cite_note-Shiseki-1)

The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of [JR West](/wiki/JR_West "JR West") [Nishi-Ōtsu Station](/wiki/Nishi-%C5%8Ctsu_Station "Nishi-Ōtsu Station") or a two-minute walk from [Ōmijingūmae Station](/wiki/%C5%8Cmijing%C5%ABmae_Station "Ōmijingūmae Station") on the [Keihan Electric Railway](/wiki/Keihan_Electric_Railway "Keihan Electric Railway") [Ishiyama Sakamoto Line](/wiki/Ishiyama_Sakamoto_Line "Ishiyama Sakamoto Line").[[1]](#cite_note-Shiseki-1)
